RESOURCE: ProtoPie to Unity: A Real-Time Iteration Guide
      CLASSIFICATION: Unclassified
      TYPE: Turtorial
      SOURCE LINK: https://www.notion.so/protopie/ProtoPie-to-Unity-A-Real-Time-Iteration-Guide-29545184b5da8051bc59c664f535921a?source=copy_link
      
      === INTERNAL NOTION NOTES ===
      
### Assets

* 3D Car Model 🚗
* Scripts (Code) 📀
* Completed Pie 🥧
* Completed Unity file 🕹️

### Introduction

The current workflow with the ProtoPie Unity plugin requires building your Unity project as a WebGL application and loading it into ProtoPie Connect stage view. You must repeat this process every time you make a change.
This tutorial shows you a much faster approach.
We'll build a bridge that lets ProtoPie send messages directly to the Unity Editor in Play Mode. Test your ProtoPie UI on your phone or computer and watch your Unity scene react instantly. No need to constantly rebuild your project.

### What We'll Build

A simple 3D car scene in Unity. With a ProtoPie UI, you'll be able to:
* Change Camera Angles: Switch between 5 different camera views.
* Change Environments: Swap between "Spring" and "Winter" environments, each with unique lighting.
* Reset the Scene: Return everything to its default state.

### Prerequisites

Before you start, make sure you have:
* Unity Hub and a Unity Editor (2022.3.xx LTS recommended).
* ProtoPie to build the UI.
* ProtoPie Connect to send messages from ProtoPie to your computer.
* A 3D car model (or use a simple cube as a placeholder).


### Part 1: Your New Unity Project

metadata.title: ProtoPie to Unity: A Real-Time Iteration Guide

pageContent:
### Part 1: Your New Unity Project

Let's start from a completely blank slate.
* Open Unity Hub and click New project.
* Select the 3D (URP) template. URP (Universal Render Pipeline) gives us prettier graphics out of the box.
* Give your project a name, like "ProtoPie Tutorial".
* Click Create project.
Unity will open, showing a new, empty scene. For a beginner, the interface is simple:
* Hierarchy (Left): A list of all objects in your scene (like layers in Figma or Sketch).
* Scene (Center): Your 3D visual workspace.
* Project (Bottom): Your project's file folder. All your assets (models, scripts, etc.) live here.
* Inspector (Right): Shows the properties (details) of whatever object you have selected.


### Part 2: Import the Socket.IO Package

We need to add a package that listens for messages from ProtoPie Connect. ProtoPie uses the Socket.IO messaging protocol.
* In Unity, go to Window > Package Manager in the top menu.
* Click the + icon in the top-left corner.
* Select "Add package from git URL..."
* Paste this URL and click Add:
https://github.com/itisnajim/SocketIOUnity.git
* Unity will download and install the package—this is the receiver library that listens for ProtoPie's messages.


### Part 3: Building the Unity Scene (The "Stage")

Now, let's build the visual part of our project.

### 1. Create Folders

Let's stay organized. In the Project window (at the bottom), right-click Assets and choose Create > Folder. Create these three folders:
* Scripts
* Lighting Profiles
* Models

### 2. The Ground and Car

metadata.title: ProtoPie to Unity: A Real-Time Iteration Guide

pageContent:
### 2. The Ground and Car

* Ground: In the Hierarchy window, right-click and choose 3D Object > Plane. This is our ground. You can scale it up by selecting it and changing the Scale values in the Inspector.
* The Car: Drag your 3D car model from your computer into your new Models folder. Then, drag the model from the Models folder into the Hierarchy to add it to the scene.
* No car model? No problem. Right-click in the Hierarchy and choose 3D Object > Cube. Select the Cube, and in the Inspector, change its name to Car.
* Adjust car/plane size: Your scene should now have the car and plane. Adjust the plane size to fit the 3D car model. You can use the gizmo (orange square) or change the scale values in the Inspector.

### 3. The Environments

We'll create two simple environments to toggle between.
* In the Hierarchy, right-click and choose Create Empty. Name it _Environment_Spring.
* Right-click _Environment_Spring and choose 3D Object > Cylinder. This will be a tree trunk. Move and scale it as you like.
* Right-click the Cylinder and Duplicate it a few times. Move them around to look like a small spring forest.
* In the Hierarchy, right-click and choose Create Empty. Name it _Environment_Winter.
* Right-click _Environment_Winter and choose 3D Object > Sphere. This is a "snowman." Duplicate it and stack it.
* Crucial Step: Select both _Environment_Spring and _Environment_Winter in the Hierarchy and disable them by unchecking the box next to their name in the Inspector. Our scripts will turn them on and off.

### 4. The Camera Targets

metadata.title: ProtoPie to Unity: A Real-Time Iteration Guide

pageContent:
### 4. The Camera Targets

We need to save our 5 camera positions.
* In the Hierarchy, create an empty object named _CameraTargets. Check that the position values are x=0, y=0, z=0.
* Select your Main Camera in the Hierarchy. Move and rotate it in the Scene view to find your first cool angle (e.g., a front view of the car). Use the camera preview window in the bottom-right corner of the Scene view to see what the camera sees.
* Now, in the Hierarchy, right-click _CameraTargets and choose Create Empty. Name it CamTarget_1. This will make CamTarget_1 a child of _CameraTargets.
* Go to the Main Camera's Transform component in the Inspector. Click the three dots and select Copy Component.
* Go back to CamTarget_1, click the three dots on its Transform component, and select Paste Component Values. You've just saved that camera position!
* Repeat this process: Move the Main Camera to a new spot, create CamTarget_2 as a child of _CameraTargets, and paste the transform values. Do this for all 5 camera targets.

### 5. Save Your Scene

Press Ctrl+S (or File > Save). Name your scene "MainScene". The asterisk (*) in the scene tab will disappear. This is a critical step!
Your scene is now set up!

### Part 4: Adding the C# Scripts (The "Brain")

This is the logic that makes everything work. We will add four C# scripts inside our Scripts folder. Drag and drop the downloaded scripts into the folder.


### 1. LightingProfile.cs

This script is a template (a "Scriptable Object") that will hold our lighting data.

### 2. ProtoPieConnector.cs

This is the "Receiver" that listens for messages from ProtoPie.

### 3. EnvironmentManager.cs

This is the "Scene Boss" that handles switching environments and lights.

### 4. CameraDollyController.cs

This is the "Camera Operator" that moves the camera.

### Part 5: Connecting Everything (The "Wiring")

metadata.title: ProtoPie to Unity: A Real-Time Iteration Guide

pageContent:
You have the scene and the scripts. Now we just have to "wire" them together in the Inspector. This is the most important part!
* Create the Manager Object:
  * In the Hierarchy, right-click and choose Create Empty. Name it _AppManagers. This object will hold all our scripts.
* Add the Scripts:
  * Select _AppManagers. In the Inspector, click Add Component and add our three main scripts:
    * ProtoPieConnector
    * EnvironmentManager
    * CameraDollyController
* Create Lighting Profile Assets:
  * In the Project window, go to your Lighting Profiles folder.
  * Right-click and choose Create > Environments > Lighting Profile. Name it LP_Spring.
  * Create another one named LP_Winter.
  * Select LP_Spring and set its Ambient Color to a nice green and Enable Fog to false.
  * Select LP_Winter and set its Fog to true and Fog Color to a light blue.
* Create the "Default Object":
  * In the Hierarchy, right-click and choose 3D Object > Capsule.
  * In the Inspector, rename it Default_Object.
  * Position it somewhere you can see it.
* Configure EnvironmentManager:
  * Select _AppManagers.
  * Find the Environment Manager component.
  * Objects To Disable...: Click the + to add one slot. Drag your Default_Object from the Hierarchy into this slot.
  * Environment Mappings: Set the Size to 2.
    * Element 0:
      * ProtoPie Value: Spring This is the value that you will use in ProtoPie for the “send/receive” response
      * Environment Parent: Drag _Environment_Spring from the Hierarchy here.
      * Lighting Profile: Drag LP_Spring from the Project window here.
    * Element 1:
      * ProtoPie Value: Winter
      * Environment Parent: Drag _Environment_Winter from the Hierarchy here.
      * Lighting Profile: Drag LP_Winter from the Project window here.
* Configure CameraDollyController:
  * Select _AppManagers.
  * Find the Camera Dolly Controller component.
  * Target Camera: Drag the Main Camera from the Hierarchy here.

metadata.title: ProtoPie to Unity: A Real-Time Iteration Guide

pageContent:
* Configure CameraDollyController:
  * Select _AppManagers.
  * Find the Camera Dolly Controller component.
  * Target Camera: Drag the Main Camera from the Hierarchy here.
  * Camera Targets: Set the Size to 5 (for our 5 views).
    * Element 0: ProtoPie Value: View_1 | Target Transform: Drag CamTarget_1 here.
    * Element 1: ProtoPie Value: View_2 | Target Transform: Drag CamTarget_2 here.
    * Element 2: ProtoPie Value: View_3 | Target Transform: Drag CamTarget_3 here.
    * Element 3: ProtoPie Value: View_4 | Target Transform: Drag CamTarget_4 here.
    * Element 4: ProtoPie Value: View_5 | Target Transform: Drag CamTarget_5 here.
    * Change the “Move Duration” time and “Ease Curve” to a desirable value.
* Configure ProtoPieConnector:
  * Select _AppManagers.
  * Find the ProtoPie Connector component.
  * Set the Mappings list Size to 3.
  * Element 0 (For Seasons):
    * Mapping Label: Change Season
    * Message Id: ChangeSeason
    * On Receive With Value (String): Click the + button.
    * Drag the _AppManagers object itself from the Hierarchy into the object slot.
    * Click the "No Function" dropdown.
    * 🚨 CRITICAL STEP: You must select the Dynamic function, not the Static one. Look at the very top of the list for the "Dynamic string" section. Select EnvironmentManager > SwitchToEnvironment.
  * Element 1 (For Cameras):
    * Mapping Label: Change View
    * Message Id: ChangeView
    * On Receive With Value (String): Click +.
    * Drag _AppManagers into the slot.
    * From the top "Dynamic string" section, select CameraDollyController > HandleStateChange.
  * Element 2 (For Reset):
    * Mapping Label: Reset Scene
    * Message Id: Reset
    * On Receive () (the one without "Value"): Click +.
    * Drag _AppManagers into the slot.
    * Select EnvironmentManager > ResetToDefault ().

metadata.title: ProtoPie to Unity: A Real-Time Iteration Guide

pageContent:
### Part 6: Setting Up ProtoPie

* Open ProtoPie and create a new Pie.
* Add buttons for your actions: Spring, Winter, View 1, View 2, View 3, View 4, View 5, and Reset.
* Add Send responses to each button's Tap trigger:
  * Spring Button: Send | Channel: ProtoPie Connect/Studio | Message ID: ChangeSeason | Value: "Spring"
  * Winter Button: Send | Channel: ProtoPie Connect/Studio | Message ID: ChangeSeason | Value: "Winter"
  * View 1 Button: Send | Channel: ProtoPie Connect/Studio | Message ID: ChangeView | Value: "View_1"
  * Reset Button: Send | Channel: ProtoPie Connect/Studio | Message ID: Reset
  * ...and so on for all your buttons.
* Open ProtoPie Connect on your computer.
* Load your Pie into ProtoPie Connect.
* Open your Pie in the browser by selecting the browser icon


### Part 7:  Real-Time Testing

* In the Unity Editor, press the Play button at the top of the screen.
* Your Unity console should show: "🔌 ProtoPie Connector: Connected!"
* Open your Pie (via ProtoPie Connect in a browser).
* Tap the buttons on your ProtoPie UI.
You will see the camera, environments, and lighting change instantly in the Unity Editor. No building, no compiling, no waiting.


### Part 8: Embedding Your Pie in Unity with Vuplex

We'll embed the ProtoPie UI directly into the Unity scene using the Vuplex 3D WebView asset.

### 1. Prerequisites

* You must have Vuplex 3D WebView (for your platform) purchased and imported from the Unity Asset Store.
* To import, go to Assets > Import Package > Custom Package... and select the Vuplex file.

### 2. Set Up the UI Canvas

* In your Hierarchy window, right-click and choose UI > Canvas. This creates a Canvas and an EventSystem object.
* Select the Canvas object. In the Inspector, find the Canvas Scaler component and set its UI Scale Mode to Constant Pixel Size. 

### 3. Add and Resize the Vuplex WebView

metadata.title: ProtoPie to Unity: A Real-Time Iteration Guide

pageContent:
### 3. Add and Resize the Vuplex WebView

* In your Project window, search for CanvasWebViewPrefab. It's usually in a folder like Vuplex/WebView/Core/prefabs/Resources.
* Drag the CanvasWebViewPrefab from the Project window onto your Canvas object in the Hierarchy. This makes it a child of the Canvas.
* Select the CanvasWebViewPrefab. In the Inspector, find the Rect Transform component.
* Click the Anchor Presets box (the square).
* Hold down Shift + Alt and click the "stretch-stretch" icon in the bottom-right corner. This will make the web view full-screen.


### 4. Add the Vuplex Control Script

We need a script to tell Vuplex to load the ProtoPie webpage with a transparent background. 
* Add the TransparentPieInVuplex script.cs to the Scripts folder.
* Go back to ProtoPie Studio and set the background opacity of your Pie to zero.
* Save your Pie and update it on Connect using the update icon. The Pie background will change from white to black.

### 5. Configure the TransparentPieInVuplex script

* Go back to your _AppManagers GameObject in the Hierarchy.
* Click Add Component and add your new TransparentPieInVuplex script.
* You will see a new public field on the script called Webview.
* Drag your CanvasWebViewPrefab object from the Hierarchy into this Webview slot.


### 6. Find Your Pie URL 



### 7. The Final Test

Now, you have both systems working at the same time:
* The ProtoPieConnector is listening for messages.
* The Vuplex is loading the UI.
* Make sure ProtoPie Connect is running your Pie.
* Press Play in Unity.
* Your 3D car scene will appear. After a second, your ProtoPie UI will load directly on top of it with a transparent background.
* Click the buttons in the UI.
The UI will send messages to ProtoPie Connect, and your ProtoPieConnector will receive them. Your scene will react just as it did before. You now have a complete, self-contained application!

      
      === EXTERNAL SOURCE CONTENT ===

pageContent:
RESOURCE: ProtoPie Player | Testing Prototypes
      CLASSIFICATION: Unclassified
      TYPE: Documentation
      SOURCE LINK: https://www.protopie.io/learn/docs/player/getting-started
      
      === INTERNAL NOTION NOTES ===
      ProtoPie Player is a free app designed to complement ProtoPie Studio. Easily view, experience, and test prototypes on your iOS, iPadOS, or Android devices. Access prototypes stored in the cloud, save them locally, and more — from your mobile or tablet device!
Get ProtoPie Player for iOS, iPadOS, and Android devices:
Available on the App StoreGET IT ON Google Play

### Testing Prototypes with ProtoPie Player

If you're looking for a reliable and efficient way to preview and test your prototypes on mobile devices or tablets, ProtoPie Player is the perfect solution. Certain features such as input layers, sensor-based interactions, and voice prototyping work seamlessly on ProtoPie Player but may not be fully supported on web browsers.
There are various methods for opening and testing prototypes (Pies) with ProtoPie Player:
* From the Player app
  * By opening Pies stored on the cloud via the “Cloud” page inside the Player app.
  * By opening Pies locally saved in the “Saved” page inside the Player app.
* From ProtoPie Cloud, by scanning the Pie’s QR code.
* From ProtoPie Studio, by scanning the Pie’s QR code.
* By opening the Pie link on a device that has ProtoPie Player installed.
Keep reading to learn more about testing prototypes with ProtoPie Player.

### Testing Prototypes from the Player App


### Testing Prototypes Saved in ProtoPie Cloud

To view all the Pies saved on your cloud, including those in your personal and team spaces, open the "Cloud" section in the ProtoPie Player app. Ensure that you’re logged in to access the Pies.
Learn more about ProtoPie Cloud and teams.

### Testing Prototypes Saved Locally

### Testing Prototypes Saved Locally

If you want to test your prototypes offline with ProtoPie Player, you can save them within the app. Go to the "Saved" section in the ProtoPie Player app to access your locally saved prototypes. Learn more about how to save prototypes locally.

### Testing Cloud Prototypes with ProtoPie Player

To open and test prototypes saved on your cloud page using the ProtoPie Player app, follow these simple steps:
* Open a Pie that is saved on your cloud page.
* Click on the Share button
* Select the QR code option.
* Use the ProtoPie Player app to scan the QR code.

### Testing Prototypes from ProtoPie Studio with ProtoPie Player

To ensure a smooth testing process, you can preview Pies from ProtoPie Studio in the ProtoPie Player app.

### Connecting ProtoPie Player to ProtoPie Studio

To connect ProtoPie Player to ProtoPie Studio, there are three methods available:
* Scanning the QR code.
* Entering the IP address.
* Using a USB cable.
1. Connecting ProtoPie Player by Scanning the QR Code
* Ensure your computer and smart device are connected to the same WiFi network.
* Click on Device in the toolbar in ProtoPie Studio. A QR code will show.
* Tap on the Scan QR Code button in ProtoPie Player.
* Scan the QR code.
* Once connected, click Run in the ProtoPie Studio toolbar or Run in ProtoPie Player. The prototype will run on your smart device.
* Did you make any changes to your prototype? Just run the prototype again, and the changes are reflected immediately.
2. Connecting ProtoPie Player by Entering the IP Address
* Ensure your computer and smart device are connected to the same WiFi network.
* Click on Device in the toolbar in ProtoPie Studio. Your IP address will show.
* Tap on Type IP Address from the dropdown menu in ProtoPie Player.
* Enter your IP address.
* Your device will appear under New Devices. Click on Approve.
* Once connected, click Run in the ProtoPie Studio toolbar or the Run icon in ProtoPie Player. The prototype will run on your smart device.
* Did you make any changes to your prototype? Just run the prototype again, and the changes are reflected immediately.
3. Connecting ProtoPie Player Using a USB Cable
* Make sure your computer and smart device are connected using a USB cable.
  * For Android devices, enable USB debugging.
  * For Apple devices with a Windows PC, use iTunes.
* A connection will be established automatically.
* Once connected, click Run in the ProtoPie Studio toolbar or the ProtoPie Player Run icon. The prototype will run on your smart device.
* Did you make any changes to your prototype? Just run the prototype again, and the changes are reflected immediately.
Enabling USB Debugging on Android
Enable USB debugging on Android using these steps.
* 
  1. Open Settings and click on About phone.
*

Enabling USB Debugging on Android
Enable USB debugging on Android using these steps.
* 
  1. Open Settings and click on About phone.
* 
  2. Tap on Build Number 7 times, and Developer options will appear.
* 
  3. Click on Developer options.
* 
  4. Enable USB debugging.　
* 
  5. Allow USB debugging.

### Opening Prototype Links in ProtoPie Player

If you have ProtoPie Player installed on your device and open a prototype link, you’ll be prompted to view the prototype using ProtoPie Player.
Learn more about uploading and sharing prototypes with ProtoPie Cloud.

pageContent:
RESOURCE: ProtoPie Connect Plugin Starter Code Nodejs
      CLASSIFICATION: Unclassified
      TYPE: Code Repository
      SOURCE LINK: Internal Notion
      
      === INTERNAL NOTION NOTES ===
      
### README

ProtoPie Connect Plugin Starter Code for NodeJS
Everything you need to get started writing your own plugin for ProtoPie Connect.

Author: 	Jeff Clarke
Version:	1.0
Date:		4-July-2022
Copyright: 	Studio XID

NOTE: A ProtoPie Enterprise subscription is required in order to use custom plugins with ProtoPie Connect.
First Run
This code is already set up to make the Socket.io connection to ProtoPie Connect.
Run npm install to install the Socket.io library.
Start ProtoPie Connect and type npm start to launch the plugin. You should see the message "PLUGIN_STARTED" appear in Connect's console with the value "App name".
Press CTRL-C to stop the plugin, and you'll see the message "PLUGIN_STOPPED" in Connect's console.
Customization
This code is intended to be a starting point for any plugin you will write. As such, you'll want to do some basic modifications before you do anything else:
Modify the following properties in package.json:
* name
* description
* author
Modify line 22 in plugin.js:
* const PP_CONNECT_APP_NAME = "App name";
Change "App name" to whatever you'd like to call your plugin.
From here, add whatever dependencies and code you require to integrate with your product or service.
Receiving Messages
Lines 48-63 handle incoming messages. The incoming message object has three properties:
{
	messageId: <String>
	value: <String>
	time: <Time Value>
}

metadata.title: ProtoPie Connect Plugin Starter Code Nodejs

pageContent:
For example, if your Pie sends the message "HELLO" with the value "World", messageId would be "HELLO", and value would be "World". It is important to note that the value property is always passed as a String. So if you're expecting a numeric value, make sure to convert it to a Number first before doing any arithmetic operations.
Sending Megasses
You can use the function sendMessageToConnect in your code to send messages to ProtoPie Connect as follows:
sendMessageToConnect("HELLO", "From my most excellent Bridge App.");
The first parameter you supply is the name of the message, and the second parameter is the value. The value parameter is optional.
Compiling
Compiling is optional. Your plugin will work just fine if your run npm start from the command line. If you're the only person working with the plugin, compiling is an unneccesary step. However if you'd like to share the plugin with others, compiling the plugin into a single file makes it easy for others to use it.
Modify metadata.json with the name of your plugin. This name is what ProtoPie Connect will display in its plugin drop down menu. It's a good idea to use the same value you used for PP_CONNECT_APP_NAME in plugin.js.
{
	"name":"Your Plugin Name"
}

metadata.title: ProtoPie Connect Plugin Starter Code Nodejs

pageContent:
You can use a number of different tools to compile NodeJS code. One of the most popular is pkg: https://github.com/vercel/pkg
Install pkg via NodeJS as follows:
npm install -g pkg
You can compile NodeJS code for multiple operating systems in a single command. Here is an example that compiles for Windows, macOS - Intel and macOS - Apple Silicon using NodeJS runtime version 16:
pkg -t node16-win-x64,node16-macos-x64,node16-macos-arm64 plugin.js
This command generates three files:
* plugin-win-x64.exe
* plugin-macos-x64
* plugin-macos-arm64
Lastly, make the macOS versions of the plugin executable with the following command:
chmod +x plugin-macos*
Packaging for ProtoPie Connect
The ProtoPie Connect plugin format is a ZIP archive with two files in it:
* plugin.exe (Windows) / plugin (mac OS)
* metadata.json
This means you'll need to create separate ZIP files for each operating system you wish to deploy your plugin to.
* For Windows, rename plugin-win-x64.exe to plugin.exe. Create a ZIP archive with plugin.exe and metadata.json.
* For Mac - Intel rename plugin-macos-x64 to plugin. Create a ZIP archive with plugin and metadata.json.
* For Mac - Apple Silicon rename plugin-macos-arm64 to plugin. Create a ZIP archive with plugin and metadata.json.
To use your plugin in ProtoPie Connect, choose "Plugin" from the top right corner. Then click "+" and browse to the appropriate ZIP archive for your OS. Start the plugin by clicking "Run". You can monitor your plugin's console output by clicking "Open in Terminal >". Stop the plugin by clicking "Stop".


### metadata.json

{
	"name":"App name"
}

### package.json

{
  "name": "protopie-connect-plugin-starter-code-nodejs",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "ProtoPie Connect plugin starter code for NodeJS",
  "main": "plugin.js",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node plugin.js"
  },
  "author": "Jeff Clarke",
  "license": "UNLICENSED",
  "dependencies": {
    "socket.io-client": "^4.5.1"
  }
}

### plugin.js

metadata.title: ProtoPie Connect Plugin Starter Code Nodejs
ProtoPie Connect Plugin Starter Code Nodejs

RESOURCE: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie
      CLASSIFICATION: Unclassified
      TYPE: Turtorial
      SOURCE LINK: https://www.notion.so/protopie/Building-Prototypes-with-Dynamic-Static-Maps-in-ProtoPie-Google-Places-and-Mapbox-IntegrationSimple-22945184b5da8072ab1dd6652db30872?source=copy_link
      
      === INTERNAL NOTION NOTES ===
      Description:
In this tutorial, you'll learn how to create a static map image that dynamically updates based on user input within your ProtoPie prototypes. We'll achieve this by integrating the Google Maps Places API (New) to fetch location coordinates and the Mapbox Static Images API to generate the map, all orchestrated seamlessly through ProtoPie Connect.


### What You'll Need:

* ProtoPie Studio (latest version recommended)
* ProtoPie Connect (latest version recommended)
* A Google Cloud Platform account with a project set up and billing enabled (billing is often required for API access, even for free tier usage).
* A Mapbox account.
* API keys for both Google Maps Platform (specifically Places API) and Mapbox.

### Part 1: Setting Up Your API Keys


### 1.1 Google Maps Platform API Key

metadata.title: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie

pageContent:
### Part 1: Setting Up Your API Keys


### 1.1 Google Maps Platform API Key

* Go to Google Cloud Platform: Visit console.cloud.google.com.
* Create a Project (if you don't have one): In the top bar, click the project dropdown and select "New Project." Follow the prompts to create your project.
* Enable Billing: Even for free tier usage, billing needs to be enabled for most Google Maps Platform APIs. Navigate to "Billing" in the left-hand navigation and set up a billing account.
* Enable APIs & Services:
  * In the left-hand navigation, go to "APIs & Services" > "Enabled APIs & Services."
  * Click "+ Enable APIs and Services."
  * Search for "Places API (New)" and enable it.
  * (Optional, but good for robust location handling): Search for "Geocoding API" and enable it. While Places API (New) often provides coordinates, Geocoding can be a useful fallback or for specific address-to-coordinate needs.
* Create API Credentials:
  * Go to "APIs & Services" > "Credentials."
  * Click "+ Create Credentials" and select "API Key."
  * Your API key will be generated. Copy this key and keep it safe. You can restrict this key later for security, but for now, just copy it.

### 1.2 Mapbox Access Token

* Go to Mapbox Account: Visit account.mapbox.com.
* Generate a Public Token: On your dashboard, you'll see your "Default public token." If you don't have one or want a new one, click "Create a token."
* Copy this token. This is your Mapbox Access Token.

### Part 2: Designing Your ProtoPie Prototype


### 2.1 Preparing ProtoPie Studio for Figma Import

* Open ProtoPie Studio and create a new Pie file.
* Match Canvas Size to Figma Frame: Before importing, ensure your ProtoPie canvas size matches the size of the frame you intend to export from Figma. This prevents scaling issues and ensures a perfect 1:1 import.

### 2.2 Importing Your Design from Figma

metadata.title: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie

pageContent:
### 2.2 Importing Your Design from Figma

* Prepare your design in Figma: Ensure your Figma file contains all necessary layers and organize them into a frame. Alternatively, you can start with the provided Figma file.
* Export from Figma:
  * In the lower toolbar, click the "Actions" icon.
  * From the "Actions" menu, select the "Plugins and Widgets" tab.
  * Search for "ProtoPie" in the search bar.
  * Click on the ProtoPie plugin.
  * When the ProtoPie plugin window opens, select "Export." Your Figma design will now be imported directly into the already open ProtoPie Studio file, appearing on your canvas.


### 2.3 Basic UI Setup (Building or Renaming Layers)

Whether you imported from Figma or are building directly, ensure your layers are set up as follows:
* Identify/Create an Input Layer:
  * Add a "Text Input" layer. Name it something descriptive like locationInput. This is where the user will type their desired location.
  * (Optional): Move the locationInput layer into a Search container for better organization.
  * Configure the Text Input parameters based on your preferences. The template project uses these settings:
    * Size: 226 x 40
    * Radius: 8
    * Placeholder color: D0D0D0
    * Box Fill: FFFBFB
  
* Identify/Create an Image Layer:
  * Locate your "Image" layer. This will serve as your map placeholder. Name it mapImage.

### Part 3: Connecting ProtoPie to Google Places API (via ProtoPie Connect)

This is where ProtoPie Connect shines. We'll use its API plugin to make the HTTP request to Google Places.

### 3.1 Introduction to Google Places API (New)

metadata.title: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie

pageContent:
This is where ProtoPie Connect shines. We'll use its API plugin to make the HTTP request to Google Places.

### 3.1 Introduction to Google Places API (New)

Before we dive into the setup, let's understand why the Google Places API (New) is crucial for our project. This API is Google's latest evolution for accessing rich location data, offering enhanced performance, improved data quality, and more flexible pricing models compared to its predecessors. It's designed to provide comprehensive information about places, including businesses, points of interest, and geographic locations.
For our specific use case, we'll be using its "Text Search" capability. This allows us to send a simple text query (like "Eiffel Tower" or "New York City") and receive detailed information about matching places, critically including their precise latitude and longitude coordinates. This is the essential first step in transforming a user's typed location into something a map service can understand.

### 3.2 Why ProtoPie Connect?

Making direct API calls from your Pie prototype isn't possible. This is where ProtoPie Connect becomes indispensable. ProtoPie Connect acts as a powerful bridge, allowing your ProtoPie prototypes to communicate with external services and applications, including making HTTP requests to APIs like Google Places. It handles the complex network communication, receives the raw data, and then sends it back to your Pie prototype for you to process and display. Without Connect, our prototype wouldn't be able to fetch the location coordinates we need.

### 3.3 Sending the Request from your Pie

First, we'll ensure our prototype continuously captures the user's input and saves it in a variable.

metadata.title: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie

pageContent:
### 3.3 Sending the Request from your Pie

First, we'll ensure our prototype continuously captures the user's input and saves it in a variable.

* Create a new Text Variable:
  * Go to the Variables Panel (bottom-left of ProtoPie Studio).
  * Create a new Text Variable (For All Scenes/Global variable) and name it searchInput. This variable will hold the text typed by the user.
  * Change your variable type from "Number" to "Text" in the properties panel.
* Capture User Input with a Detect Trigger:
  * Add a "Detect" Trigger.
  * Target: locationInput → Text (This monitors the text property of your input field).
  * Inside this "Detect" Trigger, add an "Assign" Response:
    * Target: searchInput variable
    * Formula: locationInput.text
  * Explanation: As the user types, the locationInput.text property changes. The Detect trigger fires, and this Assign response updates our searchInput variable with the latest text. Use the Debug feature to monitor the variable in real-time.
  * Open Preview to test the Pie. As you type in the input field, you'll see the variable updating in real-time in the debug view.
    
    *Remember to rename your triggers and responses with descriptive labels for better  organization and easier identification in your project.

### 3.4 Triggering the API Request with the Return Key

metadata.title: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie

pageContent:
### 3.4 Triggering the API Request with the Return Key

* Add New Global Variables to the Scene:
  * googleMapsRequestBody (Text Variable, For All Scenes): This variable will hold the complete JSON request body string for the Google Places API (New). Initialize it with the following exact string:JSON
    {
  "textQuery": "<INPUT QUERY>",
  "languageCode": "en",
  "regionCode": "kr"
}
    * Explanation:
      * "textQuery": The field for our user's search query.
      * "<INPUT QUERY>": This is a unique placeholder that we will replace with the actual user input.
      * "languageCode": "en": Specifies the language for the response (English).
      * "regionCode": "kr": Specifies the region bias for the search results (South Korea, based on your current location). Adjust this as needed.
  * inputQuery (Text Variable, For All Scenes): This variable will serve as a placeholder within our API request body string, which we'll dynamically replace. Initialize it with <INPUT QUERY>.
* Add a "Return" Trigger:
  * Select the locationInput layer.
  * Add a "Return" Trigger. We'll use the "Return" key on the keyboard to initiate the search, which is a common interaction pattern for input fields.
  * Explanation: This trigger activates when the user presses the 'Enter' or 'Return' key on their keyboard (physical or virtual).
* Prepare the Request Body and Send:
  * Inside the "Return" Trigger, add an "Assign" Response to prepare the request body:

metadata.title: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie

pageContent:
* Target: googleMapsRequestBody
    * Formula: replace(googleMapsRequestBody, inputQuery, searchInput)
    * Explanation: This crucial formula dynamically updates the googleMapsRequestBody variable. It takes the current googleMapsRequestBody string, finds every instance of the inputQuery (which is <INPUT QUERY> initially), and replaces it with the searchInput (the actual text typed by the user).
  * Add another "Assign" Response to reset inputQuery for the next search:
    * Target: inputQuery
    * Formula: searchInput
    * Explanation: After replacing the old <INPUT QUERY> placeholder with searchInput, we immediately update the inputQuery variable itself to searchInput. This means for the next search, the replace function will look for the previous search term within the googleMapsRequestBody string and replace it with the new search term. This is a clever way to manage dynamic string replacement without hardcoding the placeholder for every search.
  * Add a "Send" Response to initiate the API call:
    * Channel: ProtoPie Connect/Studio
    * Message: searchTextRequest (This is the message ProtoPie Connect will listen for).
    * Value: Select Formula and use googleMapsRequestBody.
    * Explanation: We are sending the complete, dynamically prepared JSON request body string to ProtoPie Connect.

### 3.5 Receiving and Parsing the Response in ProtoPie Studio and Generating the Mapbox URL

metadata.title: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie

pageContent:
Once ProtoPie Connect successfully retrieves the data from Google Places, it sends it back to the Pie. In this part of our API workflow, we'll receive this data, parse the coordinates, and then dynamically construct the Mapbox Static Images API URL to update our map image.
* Review or Create Necessary Text Variables (For All Scenes):
  * apiResponseRaw (Text Variable): This variable receives the raw JSON text from ProtoPie Connect.
  * latitude (Text Variable): This variable will store the extracted latitude value.
  * longitude (Text Variable): This variable will store the extracted longitude value.
  * mapBox_URL (Text Variable): This variable will hold our base Mapbox URL string with placeholders. Initialize this variable with your Mapbox base URL, including clear placeholders for latitude and longitude (e.g., <LAT> and <LON>):
    https://api.mapbox.com/styles/v1/mapbox/streets-v12/static/<LON>,<LAT>,14,0/1200x800?access_token=YOUR_MAPBOX_TOKEN
    (Note: Remember to replace YOUR_MAPBOX_TOKEN with your actual Mapbox Access Token.
  * currentlatitudePlaceholder (Text Variable): Initialize it with <LAT>. This variable will hold the last latitude placeholder/value that was inserted into mapBox_URL.
  * currentLongitudePlaceholder (Text Variable): Initialize it with <LON>. This variable will hold the last longitude placeholder/value that was inserted into mapBox_URL.
  * isMapReady (Number Variable): Initialize it with 0. This variable will act as a flag to ensure both latitude and longitude have been updated before the map image is loaded.
* Add a "Receive" Trigger:
  * Channel: ProtoPie Connect/Studio
  * Message: searchLocationReceived
  * Assign to Variable: Check this box and select apiResponseRaw. Make sure its type is Text.
* Add a "Detect" Trigger for apiResponseRaw:
  * Target: apiResponseRaw
  * Inside this Detect trigger, add "Assign" Responses to extract latitude and longitude from the JSON:
    * Assign  (for latitude):
      * Target: latitude

metadata.title: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie

pageContent:
* Target: apiResponseRaw
  * Inside this Detect trigger, add "Assign" Responses to extract latitude and longitude from the JSON:
    * Assign  (for latitude):
      * Target: latitude
      * Formula: parseJson(apiResponseRaw, "places.0.location.latitude")
      * Explanation: This formula parses the JSON response from the Places API (New). places.0 refers to the first place in the places array (as the API can return multiple results). location.latitude is the precise path to the latitude value within the JSON structure.
    * Assign  (for longitude):
      * Target: longitude
      * Formula: parseJson(apiResponseRaw, "places.0.location.longitude")
      * Explanation: Similar to latitude, but for the longitude value.
* Add a separate "Detect" Trigger for longitude:
  * Target: longitude
  * Inside this Detect trigger:
    * Assign  (Generate Mapbox URL - Longitude first):
      * Target: mapBox_URL
      * Formula: replace(mapBox_URL, currentLongitudePlaceholder, longitude)
      * Explanation: This will take the mapBox_URL template and replace the currentLongitudePlaceholder (which starts as <LON> and then becomes the previous longitude) with the newly acquired longitude.
    * Assign  (Update currentLongitudePlaceholder):
      * Target: currentLongitudePlaceholder
      * Formula: longitude
      * Explanation: This updates our placeholder variable. For the next search, this variable will contain the longitude that was just inserted into the URL, allowing the replace function to find it and substitute it with the new longitude.
    * Assign  (Update isMapReady):
      * Target: isMapReady
      * Formula: isMapReady + 1
* Add a separate "Detect" Trigger for latitude:
  * Target: latitude
  * Inside this Detect trigger:
    * Assign  (Generate Mapbox URL - Latitude second):
      * Target: mapBox_URL 
      * Formula: replace(mapBox_URL, currentLatitudePlaceholder, latitude)

metadata.title: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie

pageContent:
* Inside this Detect trigger:
    * Assign  (Generate Mapbox URL - Latitude second):
      * Target: mapBox_URL 
      * Formula: replace(mapBox_URL, currentLatitudePlaceholder, latitude)
      * Explanation: Now, this takes the mapBox_URL (which already has the updated longitude from the previous detect trigger) and replaces the currentLatitudePlaceholder (which starts as <LAT> and then becomes the previous latitude) with the newly acquired latitude. This completes the dynamic construction of the Mapbox URL.
    * Assign  (Update currentLatitudePlaceholder):
      * Target: currentLatitudePlaceholder
      * Formula: latitude
      * Explanation: This updates our placeholder variable. For the next search, this variable will contain the latitude that was just inserted into the URL, allowing the replace function to find it and substitute it with the new latitude.
    * Assign (Update isMapReady):
      * Target: isMapReady
      * Formula: isMapReady + 1
* Add a separate "Detect" Trigger for isMapReady (to load the image):
  * Target: isMapReady
  * Inside this Detect trigger:
    * Condition: isMapReady = 2. This condition ensures that mapBox_URL has been updated with both the latitude and longitude.
      * Respond with a "Image" Response:
        * Target: mapImage (your image placeholder layer).
        * Source: Select Formula.
        * Formula: mapbox_URL
      * Assign  (Reset isMapReady):
        * Target: isMapReady
        * Formula: 0
        * Explanation: Set isMapReady variable to zero for the next search.

metadata.title: Building Prototypes with Dynamic Static Maps in ProtoPie: Google Places and Mapbox IntegrationSimple Navigation Pie

pageContent:
### 3.6 Configuring the API Plugin in ProtoPie Connect

Now, let's configure ProtoPie Connect to handle the searchTextRequest message and interact with the Google Places API (New).
* Save your ProtoPie file either locally or in the cloud.
* Open your saved .pie file in ProtoPie Connect.
* Click the "Plugin" button in ProtoPie Connect (top right).
* Select "API" from the plugin list and tap the "+" button.
* Configure the API Plugin:
  * Method: POST
  * URL: https://places.googleapis.com/v1/places:searchText
  * Header:JSON
    {
  "Content-Type": "application/json",
  "X-Goog-Api-Key": "YOUR_GOOGLE_API_KEY",
  "X-Goog-FieldMask": "places.location"
}
    (Remember to replace YOUR_GOOGLE_API_KEY with your actual Google API Key.)
  * Body: Leave empty, as the body will be dynamically generated from the Pie.
  * Message From Pie: Select searchTextRequest (the exact message sent from ProtoPie Studio).
  * Check Override and set BODY with Message Value.
  * Message To Pie: Enter searchLocationReceived (the exact message ProtoPie Studio will receive).
  * Click "Activate."

### Part 4: Testing Your Prototype

* Ensure your Pie is updated in ProtoPie Connect (if you made changes after initial load).
* Ensure ProtoPie Connect is running and your Pie is loaded in it with the API plugin activated.
* Run the prototype in ProtoPie in a web browser via ProtoPie Connect's preview link.
* Type a location (e.g., "Eiffel Tower," "Times Square") into the input field and press the 'Enter' key.
* The map image should reload with the new static map reflecting your searched location.
Tip: Since the static map always loads with the searched location at its center, placing a marker UI element at the center of your ProtoPie canvas will always visually point to that location on the updated map.

### Additional Improvements:

* You can enhance the user experience by adding features such as loading screens and map drag interactions

pageContent:
RESOURCE: Open AI API - Chat Completions & Image Generation
      CLASSIFICATION: Unclassified
      TYPE: Turtorial
      SOURCE LINK: https://www.notion.so/protopie/Open-AI-API-Chat-Image-21c45184b5da80f6bfaded756278b7b4?source=copy_link
      
      === INTERNAL NOTION NOTES ===
      Open AI API — Chat Completions & Image Generation
Mickey Oh, Jun 20, 2025, Project Development Documentation v5

### 1. Objective

Develop ProtoPie prototypes that showcase the usage of Open AI API with ProtoPie connect, specifically focusing on "Chat Completions" and "Image Generation" models.

### 2. Context


Current ChatGPT is limited to generating one image per request. When users request to create multiple images, ChatGPT still returns a single image, combining multiple images into a single grid image. Additionally, it can generate only up to four variations of a concept per prompt, and its feedback system offers limited interaction with user preferences, resulting in low user engagement.
This demo enhances this experience by enabling the separate and simultaneous generation of multiple images and introducing a more intuitive, user-friendly feedback system for generated AI image creation.

### 3. Set up

I only used ProtoPie Connect to set up the Open AI API configuration and send/receive to communicate between the pie and the server.

### 3.1. Text Generations

metadata.title: Open AI API - Chat Completions & Image Generation

pageContent:
### 3. Set up

I only used ProtoPie Connect to set up the Open AI API configuration and send/receive to communicate between the pie and the server.

### 3.1. Text Generations

For general AI chat experience, there are two different API endpoints: Chat Completions API & Responses API. I used the Chat Completions endpoint which generates a model response from a list of messages comprising a conversation. Below is the ProtoPie Connect plugin configuration for Chat Completions endpoint.
* Method / URL
POST https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions
* Header
{"Content-Type": "application/json", "Authorization": "Bearer {$YOUR API KEY}"}
* Body
{ "model": "gpt-4o-mini", "messages": "<<User Message>>" }
Note that the Responses endpoint requires different configuration, which mainly differs in the Body request. A completion requires a messages array, but a response requires an input (string or array). Same for getting and parsing responses, the fields differ. The Responses API returns output, while the Chat Completions API returns a choices array.

### 3.2. Image Generation

metadata.title: Open AI API - Chat Completions & Image Generation

pageContent:
### 3.2. Image Generation

Like text generation, Open AI provides two types of API for generating images: Image API & Responses API(+ built-in image generation tool). The main difference is Responses API allows generating images as part of conversations or multi-step flows while the Image API is good for generating or editing a single image from one prompt.
Since I initially planned to build a prototype that works the same as the original Chat GPT, in which the user can create images in between the conversations, I tried using the Responses API. However, the Responses API returns a base64-encoded image, which ProtoPie Media response can’t process. Thus, I used the Image API in this demo, which supports dall-e-2 and dall-e-3 models that return the URL of the image.
Below is the ProtoPie Connect plugin configuration for the Image API endpoint.
* Method / URL
POST https://api.openai.com/v1/images/generations
* Header
{"Content-Type": "application/json", "Authorization": "Bearer {$YOUR API KEY}"}
* Body
{
"model": "dall-e-2",
"prompt": "<<USER_REQUEST>>",
"response_format": "url",
"n": <<n>>
}
It’s important to note that the model should always be set to dall-e-2 as the goal of this demo is to enable separate and simultaneous generation of multiple images. Dall-e-3 can only generate one image per request, and gpt-image-1 only returns base64-encoded images.

### 3.3. JSON Parsing


### 3.3.1. Escaping Special Characters

metadata.title: Open AI API - Chat Completions & Image Generation

pageContent:
### 3.3. JSON Parsing


### 3.3.1. Escaping Special Characters

When sending a request(query) to API, the ProtoPie built-in function replace() is used to include a user message in the request by replacing the certain phrase from the pre-made body template. However, this causes JSON syntax concatenation issues when the user includes quotations(“”) in their inquiries.
One solution(not ideal) to this problem is to use an assign response to create the entire Body request with escaping the special characters. The following syntax escapes any double quotes in the user's input (“”) by turning them into \” so that they don’t break the JSON syntax. It’s also safe to ensure that the GPT would not return any markdown formatting, by explicitly stating it in the request.
"{\"model\":\"gpt-4o-mini\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"developer\",\"content\":\"Respond in plain text only. You MUST AVOID using Markdown formatting like **bold**, *italic*, or other special characters.\"},{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"" + replace(`message`.text, "\"", "\\\"") + "\"}]}"

### 3.3.1. Chat Completions Response Parse

parseJson(gptResponse,"choices.0.message.content")

### 3.3.2. Image Generations Response Parse

parseJson(gptResponse, "data." + text(n) +".url")

### 3.3.3. Preserving Chat Context

By default, neither the Chat Completions API nor the Responses API retains previous chat history between requests. To preserve context in a conversation, a common workaround is to include the entire chat history, both user messages and GPT responses, in each new request. This allows the model to understand the ongoing conversation as if it were continuous.

### 4. Tips

metadata.title: Open AI API - Chat Completions & Image Generation

pageContent:
### 4. Tips

The following tips aren't specific to this demo, but they’re insights I found helpful during the process and applicable to general ProtoPie workflows and best practices.
* The foundational premise of ProtoPie is that an object must already exist in the scene in order to be shown later using triggers and responses. For example, in a chat experience, message placeholders should be pre-created and initially hidden, typically using Opacity or Scale. When the user sends a new message, the corresponding placeholder becomes visible with the user message. A good way to manage this setup is by using Components, since chat messages are repetitive elements that only differ in content.
* When using Send and Receive, it's recommended to pair Detect along with the receive. The reliability of Send and Receive can be affected by factors like internet speed and device performance, which may occasionally prevent the responses under Receive from triggering due to latency. A more reliable approach is to use Assign to Variable when receiving data, then use Detect on that variable to trigger the desired responses. This ensures that the responses are always executed only after the data has been successfully received and updated.
* When using Move inside a Component, and the target element is inside a container from the scene, the position values should match its original location in the scene, not the values shown inside the component. This is because the element’s position changes relative to the container, so using the scene-level X and Y ensures it moves to the correct location.

### 5. Success Metrics

metadata.title: Open AI API - Chat Completions & Image Generation

pageContent:
### 5. Success Metrics

* Chat context retention — context-aware responses
* Component-based message handling
* Latency Handling for stable API communication
* Auto-scrolling chat — scrolls to the latest message automatically
* Responsive layout behavior — the interface adapts to changes in input field focus, shrinking or expanding to accommodate keyboard input
* Intuitive AI generated image feedback system
* Auto-Layout implemented

### 6. Future Enhancements

* Typing Indicator Animation
* Storing chat history in local storage or state variables — remains accessible even after refresh or prototype reset
* Integrate both chat completions and image generation model into single pie

RESOURCE: API Plugin Tutorial for Beginners
      CLASSIFICATION: Unclassified
      TYPE: Turtorial
      SOURCE LINK: https://www.protopie.io/blog/api-plugin-tutorial-for-beginners
      
      === INTERNAL NOTION NOTES ===
      
### API Plugin Tutorial for BeginnersDiscover the key features and use cases of the API plugin in this beginner tutorial.Jeff Clarke, UX Designer & ProtoPie EducatorSeptember 19, 2023

ProtoPie Connect offers the nearly limitless capability to take your prototyping beyond the digital screen, and in true ProtoPie fashion, ProtoPie Connect includes several plugins to make this really easy. One such plugin is the new API Plugin which allows connection to common data services without the need to write any code. This article will take you through a few examples, showing you how simple it is to start working with real data in your prototypes.

### Download ProtoPie Connect

To follow along with this tutorial, you’ll need to download and install ProtoPie Connect. If you already have ProtoPie Connect installed, make sure to update it. You’ll need the latest version to complete all of the steps in this tutorial. As ProtoPie Connect is only available to paid plans, you’ll need either a Pro or Enterprise license.

### What is an API?

API stands for “Application Programming Interface,” and it’s the common way remote systems communicate with each other. In particular, the ProtoPie Connect plugin works with RESTful APIs. It’s not important that you understand exactly what RESTful means, but it’s enough to know that many MANY APIs you’ll find on the web are of this type and, therefore, can be used with the plugin.
A RESTful API transaction occurs in two parts:
* You request a remote system’s URL asking for some data. Often this request is accompanied by some parameters that better describe what you are looking for.
* The remote system responds with some data or an error in case things go wrong. This response is usually formatted as JSON.

### What is JSON?

JSON is a standard way of formatting information. JSON is convenient because it is text-based and, therefore, readable. This format makes it easy to understand and use. This article assumes you already have some familiarity with JSON, but if you need a primer, many great resources on the web can quickly get you up to speed. Here is one, for example.

### A Basic Example Using the API Plugin

This example is as straightforward as it gets. Download the Useless Fact Machine starter Pie below and follow along.
Starting Pie
Open the Pie in Studio. If you preview this Pie, you’ll see that all of the interactions and animations are complete. Drag the coin above the handle of the gumball machine and let go.
Drag the coin above the handle of the gumball machine and let go.
We’re going to complete the last step, and that is to make a request to an API that will give us back a useless fact. We’ll then take that fact and display it as if it were printed on the gumball.
The API we’re using is a freely available internet API aptly named “Useless Facts.” Details of the API can be found here. The specific request URL (or endpoint) we’ll be using is this one.
Go ahead and click it! You’ll see you get a response that looks something like this in your browser:
“Useless Facts” request URL.
This is the response in JSON format. Notice how even though it is just text, it’s not super easy to read. So here’s a pro tip! Install the JSON Formatter plugin for Google Chrome. Once the plugin is installed, Google will automatically format the gibberish from above into this:
JSON Formatter plugin for Google Chrome.
JSON, at its most fundamental, is a collection of key and value pairs separated by a colon character. The text to the left of the colon is the key, and the text to the right of the colon is the value. For our purposes, we’re interested in the key named “text” — the value of this key is our random useless fact!
Sign up for the latest API prototyping news

### Configuring the API Plugin

Working with data in ProtoPie involves three steps:
* Configure the plugin in ProtoPie Connect.
* Author your Pie to Send a message to trigger the API request and to Receive a message with the API’s response.
* Run the Pie through ProtoPie Connect to see it in action.
Let’s focus on this first step.
1. Start ProtoPie Connect
2. Click on Plugins in the top-right, then on API.
Click on Plugins in the top-right, then on API.
3. You’ll see something that looks like this:
Configuring the API Plugin.
4. Configure it as follows:
* In the URL field, fill in the request URL to the API: https://uselessfacts.jsph.pl/api/v2/facts/random
* Further down under the section named Connect API with Pie, fill in the Message from Pie field with GET_USELESS_FACT.
* Fill in the Message to Pie field with RETURN_USELESS_FACT.
* Click on Activate.
* To verify that everything is working, click on Test Request. You should see a message that says “Test Success.” If you don’t, go back and make sure you’ve entered everything exactly as I have above.
The API plugin is configured and ready to use!
That’s it! The API plugin is configured and ready to use!
NOTE: The messages we used — GET_USELESS_FACT and RETURN_USELESS_FACT — are completely made up. You can use any message you want here. You’ll want to ensure you use the same messages in your Pie file (see the next step).

### Author your Pie to use the Plugin

Return to ProtoPie Studio. Notice the Trigger named Touch Up “Nickel.” This trigger fires when you release the coin after dragging it.
The Touch Up “Nickel" trigger fires when you release the coin after dragging it.
Add a Send Response under this trigger. Configure it as follows:
* For the Channel, select ProtoPie Studio. Any time you want to communicate through ProtoPie Connect, you need to use this channel.
* For the Message, use the same thing you configured in the plugin in the Message from Pie field. In our case, we used GET_USELESS_FACT.
Configuring the Send response.
This Send will fire when we drop the coin in the machine and trigger the API Plugin to make the request to the Useless Facts API.
Now we need to set up a mechanism to receive and use the response from the API. Add a Receive Trigger. Configure it as follows:
* Once again, use ProtoPie Studio as the Channel.
* For the Message, use the same message you configured in the Message to Pie field in the plugin. In our case, we used the message RETURN_USELESS_FACT.
The actual JSON response will be included as the value of the message. To use it, we need to assign it to a variable. We don’t have one yet, so let’s create one:
* In the bottom-left of ProtoPie Studio, click on the Variables tab to open the variables panel.
* Click the + icon in the panel's top-right to create a new variable. Since we only have one scene in our Pie, it doesn’t matter if you choose For All Scenes or For This Scene.
* Name the variable something appropriate. I’ll use the name apiResponse.
* Since the API responds with JSON — essentially text — we must ensure the variable is a Text-type. In the variable’s properties in the top-right of Studio, click Text.
In the variable’s properties in the top-right of Studio, click Text.
Now return to the Receive Trigger, and check the box beside Assign to Variable. In the new drop-down list that appears, select the variable you just made.

Now return to the Receive Trigger, and check the box beside Assign to Variable. In the new drop-down list that appears, select the variable you just made.
Return to the Receive Trigger, and check the box beside Assign to Variable.
At this point, our Pie will have the response to the API stored in the Text variable apiResponse. Now let’s use it!
At the top of the layer stack, there is a Text layer named Random Fact. We will fill in that text layer with the value of the “text” key from our API response.
* Add a Text Response under the Receive Trigger you just created.
* Select the Text layer Random Fact from the drop-down list.
* Choose Formula from the drop-down list under Content.
* Use the following formula:
  * parseJson(apiResponse, "text")
Use the following formula: parseJson(apiResponse, "text").

### What did we do?

We used the parseJson() function to extract data from the JSON response stored in our apiResponse variable. If this is the first time you’re seeing this function in action, be sure to read up on the docs here. The parseJson() function is essential for working with data returned from the API Plugin, and we’ll be using it again through the more advanced examples in part 2 of this series.
Save your Pie somewhere convenient on your computer. We’re ready for the final step!

### Preview in ProtoPie Connect

For the Pie to retrieve the data, the Pie needs to be running through ProtoPie Connect. If you tried previewing the Pie in Studio, you’d have noticed that it looks like nothing has changed. This is because Studio has no knowledge of the ProtoPie Connect or the API Plugin.
* Return to ProtoPie Connect and drag the saved Pie into the left side of ProtoPie Connect’s interface.
Drag the saved Pie into the left side of ProtoPie Connect’s interface.
* Click the icon that looks like a computer monitor. This will open the Pie in the desktop web view.
* Drag the coin onto the machine and watch the magic unfold!
Drag the coin onto the machine and watch the magic unfold!
* Notice how the flow of messages is displayed in ProtoPie Connect. This is useful for troubleshooting bugs in your interaction.
The Message flow is useful for troubleshooting bugs in your interaction.
That’s all for now. Check out the API Plugin Advanced Tutorial, which will show you a couple of more advanced examples.
Completed Pie

### Enhance your designs with ProtoPie's API plugin

Harness the full potential of your designs by seamlessly integrating databases and APIs. Experience the cutting-edge features of ProtoPie by trying it for free today.

      
      === EXTERNAL SOURCE CONTENT ===

RESOURCE: API Plugin Advanced Tutorial
      CLASSIFICATION: Unclassified
      TYPE: Turtorial
      SOURCE LINK: https://www.protopie.io/blog/api-plugin-advanced-tutorial
      
      === INTERNAL NOTION NOTES ===
      
### API Plugin Advanced TutorialDelve into more advanced examples demonstrating the API plugin, including an Ask ChatGPT tutorial.Jeff Clarke, UX Designer & ProtoPie EducatorSeptember 20, 2023

In the first part of this series, we learned about the API Plugin and the basics of how to use it. If you haven’t read it yet, it’s a good idea to check it out before continuing with this article. In this second part, we’ll look at some more practical examples.

### Example 1: Customizing the Request Based on Interaction

The basic example we explored in the first article was simple. We sent a request to the same URL every time and used the response. However, you’ll often find that you’ll need to customize the request URL each time. In this example, we’ll create a dictionary look-up experience where you can type in any word and pass it off to an API to retrieve its definition.
We’ll be using the Free Dictionary API for this. The API request URL looks like this:
https://api.dictionaryapi.dev/api/v2/entries/en/<word>
Notice the part of the URL at the end labeled <word>. We replace this part with the actual word we’d like to look up.
For example, to look up the definition of the word “chicken,” we’d use this URL:
https://api.dictionaryapi.dev/api/v2/entries/en/chicken
To look up the definition of the word “little,” we’d use this URL:
https://api.dictionaryapi.dev/api/v2/entries/en/little
Essentially, each word has its own unique URL. Recall from the previous article that in the ProtoPie Connect API plugin, we could only configure a single URL. In this example, we’ll look at how to deal with this situation.

### Configure the API Plugin

### Configure the API Plugin

The ProtoPie Connect API Plugin allows you to override the request URL with one supplied by your Pie. This allows us to build the URL in Studio dynamically and supply it as the value of the message the Pie sends out to trigger the API request.
In ProtoPie Connect, we’ll configure our plugin in a similar manner as we did in the first example, but with one notable difference:
* This time, leave the URL field blank. Instead, below the Message From Pie field, check the box beside Override. Leave the drop-down with the default URL.
* Fill out the Message from Pie and Message to Pie fields with whatever messages you like. I’m using DICTIONARY_REQUEST and DICTIONARY_RESPONSE respectively.
* Click Activate.
Configuring the API Plugin.
Note: You won’t be able to test the connection since the plugin does not currently know the URL to request.
Sign up for exclusive news and tips about API prototyping!

### Author your Pie

### Author your Pie

Download the starting Pie for this example, and open it in Studio.
Starting Pie
There are two Scenes in this Pie: “dictionary - home” and “dictionary - result.” We’ll do all the work in the “dictionary - result” Scene.
We’ll do all the work in the “dictionary - result” Scene.
Notice that much of the work has been done already, but pay attention to the two Send responses that are thus far unconfigured: one under a Start trigger and another under a Return trigger. Both of these will be the same, so we’ll configure one and copy it over to the other.
Configure the Send response under the Start trigger as follows:
* Use the channel ProtoPie Studio.
* Use the message you configured in the Message to Pie field in the plugin. In my case, the message I used was DICTIONARY_REQUEST.
Here is the critical part: the value you pass along with this message will be the URL that gets used by the plugin.
* Check the box beside Send Value Together.
* In the formula field that appears, use the following formula:
  * "<https://api.dictionaryapi.dev/api/v2/entries/en/>" + searchWord
In the formula field that appears, use the indicated formula.

### What did we just do?

### What did we just do?

The request URL is the same every time except for the final part, where we tack on the word we’d like to look up, which is stored in the variable searchWord. By building the URL in a formula like this, we can supply a dynamically generated URL to the plugin every single time we make a request.
We still have another unconfigured Send response in our Pie under the Return Trigger. You can configure that one the same way you did above, or you can copy the Send response you just made into the Return Trigger below. You can then delete the remaining unconfigured placeholder if you like.
Pro tip: Make sure to paste the copied Send response below the “Assign searchWord = Input.text" response. Otherwise, your Pie will make the request to the API with the previous word.
This Pie already has the Receive trigger set up to receive the response from the API and use it, so you won’t need to configure that part. But if you’re interested, take a look at the multiple Text responses that are used in that Trigger to fill in several Text layers with values from several keys in the JSON.
Multiple Text responses that are used in the trigger.

### Run the Pie in ProtoPie Connect

Like in part 1, we’ll need to run the Pie in ProtoPie Connect to see our live data in action. Drag the Pie into Connect, preview it, and try it out for yourself!
NOTE: Remember to save your Pie before you run it!
Running the Pie in ProtoPie Connect.

### Handling an Error

If you’ve tried this out, you might have encountered a situation where nothing gets returned to the Pie. This can happen if you type a word incorrectly or if you search for a word that doesn’t exist in the dictionary. The API is programmed to handle such an event, and it returns an error status code.
Here’s an example where I’ve misspelled the word “bananas.” Click on the following URL.
https://api.dictionaryapi.dev/api/v2/entries/en/bannas
Notice how the response you get is different than when you look up an actual word. If you right-click and inspect the page using Chrome’s developer tools, you’ll see that an error is reported. Specifically, it is returning the error code 404, which is the standard internet error code for “Not found.”
Error code 404 - standard internet error code for “Not found.”
The API Plugin recognizes this situation and provides different JSON back to the Pie. This is why the results come back blank. The Pie was expecting JSON formatted in a particular way, but the error JSON that is sent back doesn’t match that format.
The error JSON sent back from the plugin looks like this:
{
  "status": "Error",
  "status-code": 404,
  "response": {
    ...whatever is returned from the API...
  }
}
In our Pie, we can look for the presence of the "status" key and see if its value is “Error.” If so, we can take alternative action and display an appropriate message to the user.
In Studio, look for Trigger that Receives the message DICTIONARY_RESPONSE.
* Add a Condition
* Configure the condition as follows:
  * IF:
    * Formula:
      * parseJson(defintionJson, "status")
  * EQUALS
    * Value:
      * “Error”
Adding the condition.
There is a layer group named “no results section” in this Pie. By default, it is hidden, but under this scenario, we’ll make it visible.
* Add an Opacity response under the Condition you just created.
* Set the Opacity of the “no result section” layer group to 100.
Set the Opacity of the “no result section” layer group to 100.

* Add an Opacity response under the Condition you just created.
* Set the Opacity of the “no result section” layer group to 100.
Set the Opacity of the “no result section” layer group to 100.
* Save your work, and preview it again through ProtoPie Connect.
NOTE: When working with ProtoPie Connect and Studio simultaneously, Connect will recognize when the file has been saved and automatically reload the Pie in the web view. Just return to your browser and interact with it again.
Now when you search for something that the API can’t find, you’ll see the “No definitions found.” message.
“No definitions found.” message.

### Example 2: ChatGPT

I’m sure this is the example you all wanted to see! Yes, it is 100% possible to create a fully working experience with a conversational AI like ChatGPT in ProtoPie.
This is definitely an advanced example of how the API plugin can be used, so you’ll want to head over to the docs and familiarize yourself with how the ChatGPT API works. You’ll also need an API Key to authenticate yourself as you make requests to the API. Unfortunately, this is not free, but it is very inexpensive for exploratory uses such as this. Head over to the Open API’s pricing page to get started.

### Configure the API Plugin

### Configure the API Plugin

Like all of the previous examples, we start with setting up the ProtoPie Connect API Plugin. Configure the plugin as follows:
* Method: POST
  * We haven’t touched this configuration before. RESTful APIs work with either GET or POSTtype requests. It’s not important that you understand the difference — the docs for most APIs will tell you which one to use. A good rule of thumb is if you need to use the Body configuration in the plugin, you’ll likely need to use POST. Otherwise, GET will likely get the job done (see what I did there?).
* URL: https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions
* Header:
{
  "Content-Type": "application/json",
  "Authorization": "Bearer <YOUR API KEY>"
}
* Here’s another configuration we haven’t used. Headers allow you to specify information about the request. All requests have a header, but in the previous examples, we’ve just used the defaults under the hood. This configuration gives you the opportunity to set some header values explicitly. In this case, we’ve specified that the request’s body will be in JSON format, and we’ve specified how to authorize the request.
* Make sure to replace the part labeled <YOUR API KEY> with your actual API Key as provided by OpenAI.
* Message from Pie: ASK CHAT GPT
  * Check the box beside Override and this time select Body from the drop down — we’ll be formulating the body of the request in our Pie.
* Message to Pie: CHAT GPT ANSWER
* Click Activate
  * NOTE: If you click on Test Request, you’ll get an error. Currently, the request does not have a body, so the ChatGPT API has nothing to work with.
Configuring the API Plugin.

### Author the Pie

Download the starting Pie below and open it in ProtoPie Studio.
Starting Pie
Once again, we have an unfinished Send response named Send ASK CHAT GPT under the Trigger named Tap Send. Let’s complete it.
Setting up the Send response.
* As always, use the Protopie Studio Channel.
* Use the message ASK CHAT GPT
* As in the previous example, we want to include a value. This time, however, we will override the request’s body, not the URL.
Here’s one example of a properly formatted body parameter of a request to the Chat GPT API:
{
  "model": "gpt-3.5-turbo",
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "Why is the sky blue?"
    }
  ],
  "temperature": 1,
  "max_tokens": 256,
  "top_p": 1,
  "frequency_penalty": 0,
  "presence_penalty": 0
}
In our case, we want to replace the "content" key’s value with whatever question the user types into the input field. Essentially we’re going to make a template with a token for a bit of text that will be replaced. It can look something like this:
{
  "model": "gpt-3.5-turbo",
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "<<USER_QUERY>>"
    }
  ],
  "temperature": 1,
  "max_tokens": 256,
  "top_p": 1,
  "frequency_penalty": 0,
  "presence_penalty": 0
}
When we send the request, we’ll replace the section <<USER_QUERY>> with the actual question the user asked. Furthermore, let’s instruct ChatGPT on how we’d like our response. Let’s modify the template as follows:
{
  "model": "gpt-3.5-turbo",
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "Reply in no more than 3 sentences. <<USER_QUERY>>"
    }
  ],
  "temperature": 1,
  "max_tokens": 256,
  "top_p": 1,
  "frequency_penalty": 0,
  "presence_penalty": 0
}
This way, the user’s question will always start with “Reply in no more than 3 sentences,” followed by the actual question.
Now to create this as a template, we will create a text variable in Studio with the above content.

Now to create this as a template, we will create a text variable in Studio with the above content.
* In the lower-left corner of ProtoPie Studio, click on the Variables tab to open the variables panel if it is not already open.
* Create a variable. Name it BODY_TEMPLATE.
* Make sure it is set as a Text type. In the top-right corner of Studio’s UI, choose Text in the variable’s properties
* There is an empty text box right beneath where you selected Text type. Copy the template snippet from above and paste it into this text field. This sets the initial value of the variable.
Copy the template snippet from above and paste it into the text field.
The box isn’t big enough to show the entire template body, but don’t worry. It’s all there.
* Now return to the Send response.
* Check the box beside Send Value Together
* In the formula box that appears, use the following formula:
  * replace(BODY_TEMPLATE, "<<USER_QUERY>>", `Input`.text)
In the formula box that appears, use the indicated formula.

### What did we just do?

We used ProtoPie’s replace() function (docs here) to take the text stored in the BODY_TEMPLATE variable, look for the exact text <<USER_QUERY>>, and replace it with whatever is in the input field. Keep in mind this doesn’t modify the variable BODY_TEMPLATE. The output from the replace() function is a new bit of Text. We’re just taking that output and passing that directly along as the value of the message.
Like the previous example, the Receive Trigger that works with the API’s response has already been created for you. Take a look if you like, but you’ll see that it is very similar to our previous examples. Save your Pie — we’re done in Studio!
Drag your finished Pie into ProtoPie Connect and run it. You should have a working ChatGPT experience!
Why don't scientists trust atoms? Because they make up everything!

### Completed Pies

Dictionary Lookup Ask ChatGPT

### Bring real data into your designs with the API plugin

The ProtoPie API Plugin enables you to inject real, dynamic data into your designs, elevating your prototypes to a new level of realism and functionality. Craft interactive and data-driven prototypes that authentically embody your vision.
